edges.description: Intracellular infection context is linked to reduced MHC-I surface expression and impaired CD8+ T-cell surveillance.
Impaired MHC-I surface expression may help Brucella persist by reducing CD8+ T-cell surveillance.
Inflammatory cytokine signaling contributes to systemic febrile illness.
edges.source: Pro-inflammatory cytokine induction in epithelial and endothelial cells

nodes.description: Aminoglycoside-containing combination therapy ranked highest for efficacy in a 2024 network meta-analysis of randomized trials.

evidence.snippet: Doxycycline + Gentamicin ranked the best in efficacy (SUCRA values: 0.94), the second is Triple (SUCRA values: 0.87), and the third is Doxycycline + Streptomycin (SUCRA values: 0.78).

nodes.description: Standard oral dual antibiotic therapy used for uncomplicated human brucellosis and bacteremic disease, though relapse risk can be higher than with some aminoglycoside-containing regimens.

evidence.snippet: However, the latter is also efficacious and suitable for uncomplicated disease.
CONCLUSION: Oral doxycycline-rifampicin (DR) and IV gentamicin-doxycycline-rifampicin (GDR) regimens have similar response rates in bacteremia brucellosis.

nodes.description: Aminoglycoside-containing combination therapy with evidence of superior efficacy compared with doxycycline-rifampicin in network meta-analyses.

evidence.snippet: Brucellosis medications differ in efficacy and safety. Doxycycline + Gentamicin, Triple, and Doxycycline + Streptomycin have superior efficacy and safety.
Triple antibiotic therapy is more effective than standard dual therapy with rifampicin and doxycycline.

nodes.description: Emerging triple therapy identified as a potential strategy to reduce overall therapy failure, with very low-certainty evidence requiring confirmation.

evidence.snippet: The association of hydroxychloroquine to the dual regimen was identified as a potential strategy to prevent overall therapy failure, which is subject to confirmation in future studies.

nodes.description: Fever is a common non-specific manifestation of human brucellosis.

evidence.snippet: The disease presents with non-specific symptoms like fever, fatigue, and joint pain, often leading to complications such as endocarditis and arthritis.

nodes.description: Brucella can survive inside macrophages, establishing an intracellular niche that supports persistence and immune evasion.

evidence.snippet: Brucella abortus (Ba) is a pathogen that survives inside macrophages.

nodes.description: Brucella abortus RNA reduces IFN-gamma-induced surface MHC-I expression in epithelial, endothelial, and monocyte/macrophage contexts.

evidence.snippet: Here, we demonstrate that Ba RNA reduced the surface expression of MHC-I induced by IFN-γ in the human bronchial epithelium (Calu-6), the human alveolar epithelium (A-549) and the endothelial microvasculature (HMEC) cell lines.
In addition, we showed that Ba RNA down-modulates the MHC-I surface expression induced by IFN-γ on human monocytes/macrophages via the pathway of the Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR).

nodes.description: Brucellosis involves difficult-to-clear infection requiring prolonged combination therapy to clear infection and prevent relapse.

evidence.snippet: Brucellosis is a difficult to treat infection that requires antibiotic combinations administered over several weeks for clearance of infection and relapse prevention.

nodes.description: Brucella abortus RNA can increase IL-8 and IL-6 secretion in epithelial and endothelial cell models.
nodes.id: Pro-inflammatory cytokine induction in epithelial and endothelial cells

evidence.snippet: Contrary to our expectations, HMEC, Calu-6 and A-549 cells treated with Ba RNA had higher IL-8 and IL-6 levels compared to untreated cells.
